rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/8/8/PPPPPPPP/RNBQK: Stake a claim in the center and open lines for the bishop and queen
rnbqkbnr/pppp1ppp/8/4p3/4P3/8/PPPP1PPP/R: Develop with tempo: the knight attacks e5, so Black must respond instead of developing freely
r1bqkbnr/pppp1ppp/2n5/4p3/4P3/5N2/PPPP1P: The Italian move: the bishop takes its best diagonal, aiming at f7, the one square only Black's king defends
r1bqk1nr/pppp1ppp/2n5/2b1p3/2B1P3/5N2/PP: Prepares d4: if Black ever captures there, c3 recaptures with a pawn and White keeps the full center
r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/2B1P3/5N2/PP: Guard e4 and keep it quiet: the slow setup (castle, c3, h3) sidesteps every Two Knights trick
r1bqkbnr/pppp1pp1/2n4p/4p3/2B1P3/5N2/PPP: 3...h6 develops nothing, so open the center at once; wasted tempi get punished in open positions
r1bqkbnr/ppp2ppp/2np4/4p3/2B1P3/5N2/PPPP: 3...d6 blocks the f8 bishop and concedes the center; strike while Black is passive
r1bqk1nr/ppppbppp/2n5/4p3/2B1P3/5N2/PPPP: The bishop is passive on e7; open the center before Black finishes developing
r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b1p3/2B1P3/2P2N2: The point of 4.c3: take the whole center; after exd4 cxd4 White has the ideal d4+e4 pawn duo
r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b1p3/2B1P3/3P1N2: King safety first: in the Pianissimo White completes development before any central action
r1bqk1nr/ppp2ppp/2np4/2b1p3/2B1P3/2P2N2/: Claim the center anyway: c3 means every capture on d4 is answered by a pawn, keeping the duo
r1bqkbnr/pppp1pp1/2n4p/8/2BpP3/5N2/PPP2P: Recapture toward the center; the knight sits actively on d4 and trades favorably if challenged
r1bqkbnr/ppp2ppp/2np4/8/2BpP3/5N2/PPP2PP: Recapture toward the center; the d4 knight dominates and every trade extends White's lead
r1bqk1nr/ppppbppp/2n5/8/2BpP3/5N2/PPP2PP: The Danish idea: offer the c-pawn to rip open the center while the e7 bishop blocks Black's own kingside
r1bqk2r/ppppbppp/2n2n2/4p3/2B1P3/3P1N2/P: Castle first; Black's quiet Be7 gives no reason to hurry the center
r1bqkb1r/pppp1pp1/2n2n1p/4p3/2B1P3/3P1N2: Ignore the wing pawn and finish development; h6 costs time Black cannot afford
r1bqkb1r/ppp2ppp/2n2n2/3pp3/2B1P3/3P1N2/: Accept: 4...d5 overextends, and after exd5 Nxd5 White develops with tempo against the floating knight
r2qkbnr/ppp2ppp/2np4/4p3/2BPP1b1/5N2/PPP: Snap off e5 while the g4 bishop is committed; once the tension resolves, the pin has no target
r1bqk1nr/pppp1pp1/2n4p/2b1p3/2B1P3/2P2N2: The standing rule: answer a wasted move by taking the whole center
r1b1k1nr/pppp1ppp/2n2q2/2b1p3/2B1P3/2P2N: The queen came out too early; castle, prepare d4, and she becomes a target on f6
r1bqkbnr/ppp2pp1/2np3p/4p3/2BPP3/5N2/PPP: Two slow moves is too many: open the position and let the development lead decide
r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b5/2BpP3/2P2N2/P: The pawn recapture is why c3 was played: White gets the d4+e4 duo and the pawn hits the c5 bishop next
r1bqk1nr/ppp2ppp/2np4/2b5/2BpP3/2P2N2/PP: Recapture with the pawn: the center duo plus tempo on the c5 bishop gives White easy play
r1bqk2r/ppp2ppp/2np1n2/2b1p3/2B1P3/3P1N2: Key Pianissimo prophylaxis: take g4 away before ...Bg4 or ...Ng4 harass the f3 knight
r1bq1rk1/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b1p3/2B1P3/3P1N: The slow plan: c3 readies the d4 break and opens a retreat path for the c4 bishop
r1bqk2r/pppp1pp1/2n2n1p/2b1p3/2B1P3/3P1N: Continue the plan regardless: c3 then d4; Black's ...h6 changed nothing
r1bq1rk1/ppppbppp/2n2n2/4p3/2B1P3/3P1N2/: Same Pianissimo recipe against every setup: c3, then look for d4
r1bqkbnr/pppp1pp1/7p/8/2BnP3/8/PPP2PPP/R: With the knights traded, the queen recaptures happily: no ...Nc6 exists to kick her off d4
r1bqkb1r/ppp2ppp/2n5/3np3/2B5/3P1N2/PPP2: No need to grab anything: castle and let the floating d5 knight and open center become targets
r2qkbnr/ppp2ppp/3p4/4n3/2B1P1b1/5N2/PPP2: Break the pin immediately; Black's initiative evaporates and White finishes development with the freer game
r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/8/1bBPP3/5N2/PP3P: Greco's gambit: block the check with development and offer e4; the open lines are worth a pawn
r1bqk1nr/ppp2ppp/2np4/8/1bBPP3/5N2/PP3PP: Block with development; if Black ever takes on c3, bxc3 keeps the center and opens the b-file
r1bqk1nr/ppp2ppp/1bnp4/8/2BPP3/5N2/PP3PP: Castle and enjoy the big center; Black's setup is solid but has no active plan
r1bqk1nr/pppp1pp1/2n4p/2b5/2BNP3/8/PPP2P: Remove the c6 defender first: after the recapture Black's queenside pawns are wrecked
r1bqkbnr/pppp1pp1/7p/4n3/2BNP3/8/PPP2PPP: Sidestep and keep the f7 diagonal; the e5 knight gets kicked by f4 or traded on White's terms
r1bqk2r/ppppbppp/2n2n2/8/2BpP3/2P2N2/PP3: Push with tempo: e5 hits the knight and cramps Black before he can castle
r1bqk1nr/ppppbppp/2n5/8/2B1P3/2p2N2/PP3P: Cash the tempo before recapturing: Qd5 hits f7, forcing an awkward defense, then White rounds up c3 with a huge lead
r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n5/8/1bBPn3/2N2N2/PP3P: Keep offering: castling completes the gambit setup, and every pawn Black grabs opens another line to his king
r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n5/8/2BP4/2b2N2/P4PPP/: The point of the whole gambit: Ba3 keeps the king stuck in the center, with Re1 and d5 coming
